title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who are retired or approaching retirement and discuss this life transition. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The Next Chapter: Four People on Retirement and Life After Work

texts.A:
### Dorothy, 68, Retired Head Teacher
After thirty-five years in education, the last twelve as a headteacher, retirement arrived with a mixture of relief and a terror I hadn't adequately prepared for. [43]The first thing I noticed was the absence of structure – for thirty-five years my day had been shaped from the outside, and suddenly I had to construct it entirely from within.[/43] This proved far more difficult than I had imagined. [47]I also discovered that my sense of self-worth had been entirely tied to my professional role in ways I hadn't noticed until that role was gone – it took nearly a year to feel comfortable introducing myself without mentioning what I used to do.[/47] [51]I now mentor newly appointed headteachers through a local authority scheme, which gives me purpose without the administrative burden that had worn me down by the end of my career.[/51]
texts.B:
### Clive, 63, Recently Retired Accountant
[44]I retired eight months ago and I'd be dishonest if I said it's been entirely positive – there's a flatness to some days that I didn't anticipate and that my wife, who still works, doesn't fully understand.[/44] I had planned my finances carefully for decades but simply hadn't planned how to spend my time. [48]What has helped most is establishing non-negotiable commitments in my diary – a weekly Spanish class, swimming three mornings a week, a golf club match on Fridays – because these mean I have something to get up for regardless of how I feel.[/48] [52]I've also been reading far more than I ever did during my working life, which I suspect will turn out to be one of the real gifts of this phase once I fully adjust to its slower pace.[/52]
texts.C:
### Anita, 72, Retired Surgeon
[45]I performed my last operation at the age of sixty-eight, and the decision to stop was not entirely mine – the hospital was implementing a compulsory retirement policy and there was little I could do to challenge it.[/45] I was furious initially, and then gradually came to accept it. [49]I found an unexpected second chapter in medical writing – I've now published two books for general readers explaining complex surgical conditions, which has given me a creative dimension my clinical career never offered.[/49] I also sit on a hospital ethics committee, which keeps me engaged with medicine without the physical demands of the operating theatre.
texts.D:
### Brendan, 60, Semi-retired Architect
[46]I reduced my working week to three days at sixty, which I would recommend to anyone – it combines the identity and income of work with the time and freedom of retirement, without the psychological cliff edge of stopping all at once.[/46] The transition has been easier precisely because it has been gradual. [50]The two extra free days have revealed how much I had been neglecting – I garden properly for the first time, I've taken up pottery, and I attend a film club where the debates are fierce and the company is wonderful.[/50] I plan to reduce to two days next year and retire fully at sixty-five, by which point I hope to have built a life full enough not to miss the office.

questions.question: describes the loss of an externally imposed daily routine as the most significant initial challenge of retirement?
questions.explanation: Dorothy says the first thing she noticed was the absence of structure – for thirty-five years her day had been shaped from the outside.

questions.question: admits openly that the early period of retirement has been more emotionally difficult than expected?
questions.explanation: Clive says he would be dishonest if he said it has been entirely positive – there is a flatness to some days he didn't anticipate.

questions.question: had the timing of their retirement determined by their employer rather than by their own choice?
questions.explanation: Anita says the decision to stop was not entirely hers – the hospital was implementing a compulsory retirement policy.

questions.question: chose a gradual transition out of work in preference to stopping all at once?
questions.explanation: Brendan reduced his working week to three days at sixty, specifically to avoid the psychological cliff edge of stopping all at once.

questions.question: describes having had to reconsider how they defined their own personal identity following retirement?
questions.explanation: Dorothy says her self-worth was tied entirely to her professional role in ways she hadn't noticed, and it took a year to feel comfortable without mentioning what she used to do.

questions.question: found that scheduling fixed commitments in advance was essential to maintaining their sense of wellbeing?
questions.explanation: Clive says establishing non-negotiable diary commitments – classes, swimming, golf – gives him something to get up for regardless of how he feels.

questions.question: discovered an unexpected creative outlet that has given them a new sense of purpose after their professional career ended?
questions.explanation: Anita found a second chapter in medical writing, publishing books for general readers, which gave her a creative dimension her clinical career never offered.

questions.question: describes how the additional free time in semi-retirement has allowed them to pursue activities they had previously neglected?
questions.explanation: Brendan says the two extra days have revealed how much he was neglecting – he now gardens, has taken up pottery and attends a film club.

questions.question: continues to contribute professional expertise in a less demanding capacity than before?
questions.explanation: Dorothy mentors newly appointed headteachers through a local authority scheme, keeping her expertise active without the administrative burden of her former role.

questions.question: finds that an activity which was previously difficult to fit into their schedule has become one of the pleasures of retirement?
questions.explanation: Clive says he is reading far more than during his working life and suspects it will be one of the real gifts of this phase.
